[Event Streaming](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/event-streaming.ini.html): Event streaming is a pattern where events are continuously published to an ordered, durable log, called a stream, and consumers read from that log at their own pace, independently of producers.
[Eventual Consistency](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/eventual-consistency.ini.html): Eventual consistency is a data consistency model used in distributed systems where replicas of the same data are allowed to diverge temporarily, but are guaranteed to converge to the same value if no new updates are made.
[Chaining Exceptions](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/exception-chain.ini.html): Chaining exception is throwing again a caught exception, with a new type, and with the previous exception.
[Exception Handler](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/exception-handler.ini.html): The exception handler is a default or custom function, which is called once an exception has exhausted all available ``try-catch`` blocks.
[Exception](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/exception.ini.html): Exceptions are a mechanism for handling errors and exceptional situations in a more structured and controlled way compared to traditional error handling methods like using error codes or ``die()`` statements.
[Excimer](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/excimer.ini.html): ``Excimer`` is a low-overhead sampling profiler for PHP, developed by the Wikimedia Foundation.
[exec](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/exec.ini.html): ``exec()`` use used to execute a command with the underlying operating system.
[Execution Path](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/execution-path.ini.html): The execution path refers to the sequence in which the PHP interpreter processes and executes the code.
[Execution Time](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/execution-time.ini.html): Execution time is a phase of PHP processing, where the source code is executed.
[Execution](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/execution.ini.html): Execution refers to the ability to run code or system commands dynamically at runtime.
[EXIF (Exchangeable Image File Format)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/exif.ini.html): EXIF is a standard for storing metadata in image files, particularly JPEG and TIFF.
[Existence](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/existence.ini.html): Existence is the general concept of checking whether something is available before using it.
[Existential Type](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/existential-type.ini.html): An existential type expresses that there exists some concrete type satisfying a constraint, without naming that type at the call site.
[Exit](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/exit.ini.html): ``exit()`` and ``die()`` terminates the current execution.
[Explanation](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/explanation.ini.html): In the context of static analysis and code review, an explanation is a natural-language description of why a piece of code is flagged, what rule it violates, and how to fix it.
[Explicit Boolean Comparison](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/explicit-boolean-comparison.ini.html): The Explicit Boolean Comparison is the syntax convention that includes a literal value when using a boolean as a condition.
[Explicit](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/explicit.ini.html): A feature is explicit when its presence is represented in the source code.
[explode()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/explode.ini.html): The ``explode()`` function is used to break an array into a list of elements, based on a separator.
[Exponent](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/exponent.ini.html): Exponent is the mathematical operation to raise a number to the power of another number.
[Exponential](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/exponential.ini.html): The exponential is a mathematical function.
[expose_php](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/expose_php.ini.html): The ``expose_php`` directive displays the usage of PHP and its version in the headers of the webserver.
[Expression](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/expression.ini.html): In PHP, an expression is a combination of one or more variables, operators, constants, and functions that evaluates to a single value.
[Expressive Interface](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/expressive-interface.ini.html): An expressive interface, refers to an API design pattern that makes code more readable, intuitive, and natural to write.
[extends](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/extends.ini.html): ``extends`` is an operator of the class inheritance.
[Extension Method](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/extension-method.ini.html): An extension method, as found in C#, Kotlin, and Swift, lets a developer add a method to a type without modifying that type's source and without subclassing it:
[Extensions](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/extension.ini.html): Extensions are a custom way to add features to software.
[extract()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/extract.ini.html): ``extract()`` is a native PHP function, which creates arrays from an array containing variables and their values.
[Function-as-a-Service (FaaS)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/faas.ini.html): Function-as-a-Service, or FaaS, is a serverless execution model where individual functions are deployed, invoked on demand, and billed per execution.
[Facade](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/facade.ini.html): A facade provides a simplified interface to a complex library.
[Factory](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/factory.ini.html): A factory is a design pattern used to create objects without exposing the instantiation logic, i.
[Faker](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/faker.ini.html): A ``faker`` is a tool that generates fake data, for testing purposes.
[Fallback Function](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/fallback-function.ini.html): A fallback function is a function that replaces a function if its definition is not available in the current namespace.
[Fallback To Global](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/fallback-to-global.ini.html): The fallback to global mechanism is a PHP feature, which search for functions and global constants, first in the current namespace, then in the global namespace, before failing.
[Fallback](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/fallback.ini.html): A fallback refers to a backup plan or default behavior that is executed when the primary operation fails, is unavailable, or doesn't meet certain conditions.
[Switch Fallthrough](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/fallthrough.ini.html): A fallthrough is the absence of break, or equivalent, at the end of a switch case.
[False](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/false.ini.html): ``false`` has two usages, as PHP keyword:
[Falsy](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/falsy.ini.html): A falsy value is a value that evaluate to false, when cast to a boolean.
[Fast Artificial Neural Network (FANN)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/fann.ini.html): ``FANN``, for Fast Artificial Neural Network, is a PIE extension that wraps the FANN C library, a lightweight library for creating, training, and running multilayer feedforward artificial neural networks.
[FastCGI](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/fastcgi.ini.html): FastCGI stands for Fast Common Gateway Interface.
[Fat Controller](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/fat-controller.ini.html): A fat controller is an anti-pattern in MVC architecture where the controller class accumulates too much business logic, data transformation, and persistence concerns.
[Fat](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/fat.ini.html): Fat is an informal qualifier applied to a class, most commonly a controller or a model, that has grown too large in terms of responsibilities, methods, and lines of code.
[Fatal Error](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/fatal-error.ini.html): Fatal error is a type of PHP error, which ends the execution of the application.
[Fault Isolation](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/fault-isolation.ini.html): Fault isolation is the design property that limits the impact of a failure to the component or service where it originates, preventing it from cascading to other parts of the system.
[Feature Flag](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/feature-flag.ini.html): A feature flag, also known as a feature toggle or feature switch, is a software development technique used to enable or disable certain features or functionalities in an application or system.
[Feature Testing](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/feature-testing.ini.html): Feature testing validates that multiple components of a system work together correctly by simulating real user workflows.
[Feature](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/feature.ini.html): A software feature is a specific functionality or capability that is designed and implemented within a software application to provide a specific benefit or serve a particular purpose.
[Foreign Function Interface (FFI)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/ffi.ini.html): ``Foreign Function Interface``, also known as ``FFI`` allows loading external libraries, such as ``.
[Fibers](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/fibers.ini.html): Fibers are a low-level mechanism for lightweight concurrency, they allow to pause and resume blocks of code at specific points without blocking the rest of the application.
[First In, First Out (FIFO)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/fifo.ini.html): First In, First Out is the principle of management of a queue.
[File Extension](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/file-extension.ini.html): File extensions are the suffixes at the end of file names that indicate the type or format of the file.
[File Mode](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/file-mode.ini.html): The file mode is the configuration of the pointer when accessing to the file.
[File](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/file.ini.html): A file is a named collection of data or information that is stored and organized on a computer's storage system.
[file_get_contents()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/file_get_contents.ini.html): ``file_get_contents()`` is a PHP native function, which reads all data from a file, in one call.
[file_put_contents()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/file_put_contents.ini.html): ``file_put_contents()`` is a PHP native function, which stores data in a file, in one call.
[File System](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/filesystem.ini.html): A file system is a method or structure used by operating systems to organize, store, and manage files and data on storage devices such as hard drives, solid-state drives, USB drives, and network-attached storage.
[FileSystemIterator](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/filesystemiterator.ini.html): The ``FilesystemIterator`` class is part of the SPL Library and is used for iterating over the contents of a directory.
[filter](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/filter.ini.html): The ``filter`` extension is a built-in extension that provides an efficient way to filter, validate and sanitize data.
[Final Class Constants](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/final-class-constant.ini.html): Using the final option with class constants, the constant cannot be redefined by a child class.
[Final Keyword](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/final.ini.html): The final keyword prevents child classes from overriding a method or a constant by prefixing the definition with final.
[Finally](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/finally.ini.html): Finally is the default clause of a try-catch expression.
[Fingerprint](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/fingerprint.ini.html): A fingerprint is a compact, deterministic identifier derived by hashing a set of attributes that together describe something uniquely.
[Finite State Machine](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/finite-state-machine.ini.html): A finite state machine, also called FSM, also called a finite automaton, is a mathematical model used to design systems that can be in one of a finite number of states at any given time.
[Fire-and-forget](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/fire-and-forget.ini.html): Fire-and-forget is a pattern where a task is dispatched and the caller does not wait for its result, does not check for errors, and does not expect a return value.
[First Class Callable](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/first-class-callable.ini.html): First Class Callable Syntax is a new feature that creates a closure directly from a function, by using the triple dot operator as an argument.
[First-Class Module](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/first-class-module.ini.html): In OCaml and a few other ML-family languages, a module can itself be treated as an ordinary value:
[Fixture](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/fixture.ini.html): A fixture is a set of test data.
[Flag](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/flag.ini.html): A flag is another name for an option.
[Flame Graph](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/flamegraph.ini.html): Flame graphs are a visualization tool used to understand where a program spends its time, especially useful for performance profiling.
[Flexibility](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/flexibility.ini.html): Flexibility means that a piece of code can change the way it works, without changing the code that uses it.
[Floating Point Numbers](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/float.ini.html): Floating point numbers, also known as ``floats``, ``doubles``, or ``real numbers``, can be specified using a decimal dot and a mantis.
[Fluent Interface](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/fluent-interface.ini.html): A fluent interface, or fluid interface, is an object-oriented API whose design relies extensively on method chaining.
[fnmatch()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/fnmatch.ini.html): ``fnmatch()`` is a matching native function:
[Folklore](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/folklore.ini.html): The PHP community's folklore is all the non-language related creations, that serves as a link in the community.
[Footgun](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/footgun.ini.html): A footgun is a feature or a piece of code that makes it easy to unintentionally shoot oneself in the foot:
[fopen()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/fopen.ini.html): ``fopen()`` is a PHP native function which opens a file.
[For](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/for.ini.html): ``for()`` is a loop structure.
[foreach()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/foreach.ini.html): ``foreach()`` is the most popular type of loop.
[Form](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/form.ini.html): A form is a web page component to collect and submit information.
[Format](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/format.ini.html): Format is a set of rules to structure a piece of text, and make it parsable by a specific engine.
[Forum](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/forum.ini.html): A forum is a web application that allows users to hold structured discussions online.
[Forward Compatible](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/forward-compatible.ini.html): A forward compatible feature is a feature that will be available in future versions.
[Fossilized Methods](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/fossilized-method.ini.html): A method become fossilized when it is overwritten many times, creating a difficult update of the method signature, without impacting many parts of the code.
[FastCGI Process Manager (FPM)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/fpm.ini.html): FPM stands for FastCGI Process Manager.
[Framework](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/framework.ini.html): A framework is a library of code and practices that helps building PHP applications.
[fread()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/fread.ini.html): ``fread()`` is a PHP native function that reads up to ``length`` bytes from the file pointer referenced by ``stream``.
[Free](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/free.ini.html): Free in a software context has two distinct meanings that are often conflated:
[Front-end](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/frontend.ini.html): Front-end refers to the software that runs on the client, and not on the servers.
[fseek()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/fseek.ini.html): ``fseek()`` is a native PHP function that sets the file position indicator for a file pointer.
[ftell()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/ftell.ini.html): ``ftell()`` is a PHP native function that returns the current position of the file pointer for an open file stream.
[File Transfer Protocol (FTP)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/ftp.ini.html): FTP stands for File Transfer Protocol.
[File Transfer Protocol Secure (FTPS)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/ftps.ini.html): FTPS stands for File Transfer Protocol Secure.
[Fully Qualified Name](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/fully-qualified-name.ini.html): A fully qualified name, or a fully qualified class name, is a unique identifier for the PHP name of a constant, class, trait, interface, enum or function:
[func_get_args()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/func_get_args.ini.html): ``func_get_args()`` is a native function that returns an array containing the function's argument list.
[Function Subscripting](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/function-subscripting.ini.html): Function subscripting is the ability to apply operators like ``->``, ``?->`` or ``[<index>]`` directly on the results of a method call.
[Functions](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/function.ini.html): Functions are defined with a set of parameters, a body of code for processing, and a returned value, typed or not.
[Functional Programming](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/functional-programming.ini.html): Functional Programming, FP for short, is a programming paradigm where programs are built by combining pure functions, avoiding shared state, and minimizing side effects.
[Functor](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/functor.ini.html): A functor is a type that can be ``mapped over``.
[Future](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/future.ini.html): A future is a programming construct representing a value that is not yet available but will be resolved at some point.
[Fuzzing](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/fuzzing.ini.html): Fuzzing refers to an automated software testing technique where the application is fed random, unexpected, or malformed data to discover bugs, vulnerabilities, and unexpected behaviors.
[Generalized Algebraic Data Type (GADT)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/gadt.ini.html): A Generalized Algebraic Data Type, GADT, is an extension of algebraic data types in which each constructor of the type can return a different, more specific instantiation of the type.
[Garbage Collection](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/garbage-collection.ini.html): Garbage collection is a PHP mechanism that checks memory and frees it when it needs to store new values.
[Graphic Draw (GD)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/gd.ini.html): ``GD`` is the name of a PHP extension that can manipulate several image formats, such as GIF, PNG, JPEG, WBMP, and XPM, and draw graphics.
[Gearman](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/gearman.ini.html): ``Gearman`` is a PECL extension that provides a client for the Gearman job server, a system for distributing work across machines or processes.
[Generator](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/generator.ini.html): Generators are a way to implement simple iterators without the overhead or complexity of implementing a class that extends the Iterator interface.
[Generics](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/generics.ini.html): Generics are a style of types, where the type of a property, parameter or return is specified at the declaration level.
[get_class()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/get_class.ini.html): ``get_class()`` is a PHP native function, that returns the name of the class of an object.
[get_class_vars()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/get_class_vars.ini.html): ``get_class_vars()`` is a PHP native function that returns the default properties of a class as an associative array.
[get_object_vars()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/get_object_vars.ini.html): ``get_object_vars()`` is a PHP native function, which returns an array with the object properties as index, and their related respective values.
[Class Getter Method](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/getter.ini.html): Getters are methods used to access the values of a property.
[gettext Extension](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/gettext.ini.html): ``gettext`` is a library that provides features to translate messages for the users.
[GIF](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/gif.ini.html): GIF, for Graphics Interchange Format, is a bitmap image format developed by CompuServe in 1987.
[Git Hook](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/git-hook.ini.html): Git hooks are scripts that Git executes automatically at specific points in the version-control workflow.
[git](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/git.ini.html): Git is a distributed version control system that is widely used for tracking changes in source code during software development.
[glob()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/glob.ini.html): ``glob()`` is a function that finds pathnames matching a pattern.
[Global Code](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/global-code.ini.html): The global code is the code that doesn't belong to any method or function.
[Global Space](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/global-space.ini.html): The term ``global space`` refers to the global namespace ``\\\\`` or the top-level scope in the PHP language.
[Global State](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/global-state.ini.html): Global state refers to data that is accessible from anywhere in the application, typically stored in global variables, superglobals, or static class properties.
[Global Variables](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/global-variable.ini.html): Variables that reside in the global scope, and may be imported with the global keyword.
[global Scope](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/global.ini.html): The global scope is the top level scope.
[$GLOBALS](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/globals.ini.html): $GLOBALS is a global variable, that host all other global variables, during a PHP execution.
[Glue](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/glue.ini.html): Glue represents the piece of string that is used as separator between elements in a ``join()`` operation.
[GNU Multiple Precision (GMP)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/gmp.ini.html): ``gmp`` is a PHP extension that stands for "GNU Multiple Precision".
[God Object](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/god-object.ini.html): A ``god object`` is a term used in software development to describe a class or module that knows too much or does too much within a system.
[Golf](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/golf.ini.html): Code golf is about creating the shortest code, in bytes, to solve a given problem, in a specific language or free of choice.
[Goto](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/goto.ini.html): The ``goto`` operator is a language construct that allows to perform an unconditional jump in the flow of your code to a specified label.
[Gradual Type Checking](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/gradual-type-checking.ini.html): Gradual type checking is the progressive introduction of types in a code base.
[Graph Database](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/graph-database.ini.html): A graph database is a database server that organize data in a network, rather than as tables.
[Graph](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/graph.ini.html): A graph is a data structure consisting of a set of nodes, also called vertices, and edges that connect pairs of nodes.
[GraphQL](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/graphql.ini.html): GraphQL is a query language for APIs.
[Greenfield](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/green-field.ini.html): A greenfield project is a software development effort that starts from scratch, with no existing codebase, legacy constraints, or inherited technical debt.
[Green Thread](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/green-thread.ini.html): Green threads, also called goroutines in Go, virtual threads in Java 21+, or lightweight threads in Erlang/Haskell, are user-space threads managed entirely by the language runtime rather than the operating system kernel.
[Green](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/green.ini.html): Green describes a build, a test run, or a pipeline stage that has passed:
[gRPC (Google Remote Procedure Call)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/grpc.ini.html): ``gRPC``, short for Google Remote Procedure Call, is a high-performance framework for building APIs and connecting services:
[Habit](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/habit.ini.html): A habit, in the context of software development, is a coding practice that a developer applies automatically and repeatedly, without necessarily making a deliberate decision each time.
[__halt_compiler()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/halt-compiler.ini.html): ``__halt_compiler()`` halts the execution of the compiler.
[Handle](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/handle.ini.html): A handle represents a datastructure that has established a link with an independent resource.
[PHP Handlers](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/handler.ini.html): PHP handlers are methods which are called to customize the processing of specific events.
[Hard Coded](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/hard-coded.ini.html): Hard coded data in code happens when a value is set as a literal in the code, and can't be change through any directives or parameters.
[Hardening](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/hardening.ini.html): Hardening refers to the raise of security level in the installation of PHP, and other related technologies.
[Haru (PDF)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/haru.ini.html): ``Haru`` is a PECL extension that wraps libharu, a free, cross-platform C library for generating PDF documents from scratch.
[Hash #](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/hash-character.ini.html): ``#`` is character, used in various situations:
[Hash Comparisons](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/hash-comparison.ini.html): Cryptographic hashes are usually represented by strings.
[Cryptographic Hash](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/hash-crypto.ini.html): A cryptographic hash is an algorithm which maps a string to a fixed sized string, with a method that is difficult or impossible to revert.
[hash() Function](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/hash-function.ini.html): ``hash()`` is a PHP native function, that computes the hash of strings, with a wide range of algorithms, cryptographic or not.
[Hash Table](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/hash-table.ini.html): A hash table is a data structure that stores key-value pairs, and uses a hash function to compute an index, called a bucket, from the key, so that a value can be inserted, looked up, or deleted in average constant time ``O(1)``, regardless of the size of the table.
[Hash](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/hash.ini.html): Hash has several meanings:
[hash_equals()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/hash_equals.ini.html): ``hash_equals()`` is a built-in PHP function that compares two strings in constant time, regardless of where they differ.
[Hashing](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/hashing.ini.html): Hashing is the process of running data through a hash function, to produce a fixed-size value called a hash, or digest.
[Haystack](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/haystack.ini.html): Haystack is a classic name, used to designate a dataset where something will be searched in.
[Headers](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/headers.ini.html): Headers are HTTP response data sent to the client before any body output.
[Headless](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/headless.ini.html): A headless website, also known as a headless CMS, Content Management System, or decoupled website, is a web architecture where the frontend and backend are decoupled or separated from each other.
[Heredocs](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/heredoc.ini.html): Heredoc is a string syntax, similar to nowdoc, with internal variable interpolation.
[Heuristics](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/heuristic.ini.html): Heuristics are practical, experience-based techniques or problem-solving approaches that are not guaranteed to be perfect or optimal, but are good enough for reaching an immediate, short-term goal or solution.
[Hexadecimal Integer](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/hexadecimal-integer.ini.html): Hexadecimal integers are integers with an hexadecimal representation.
[Hexagonal Architecture](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/hexagonal.ini.html): Hexagonal architecture, also known as Ports and Adapters architecture or the Onion architecture, is a software architectural pattern that promotes the separation of concerns and emphasizes modularity and flexibility.
[hg](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/hg.ini.html): ``HG`` is another name for Mercurial, which is a distributed version control system, DVCS, similar to Git.
[Hidden State](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/hidden-state.ini.html): Hidden state is state that exists in a program and influences its behavior but is not visible or accessible from the current scope.
[Higher-Kinded Type](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/higher-kinded-type.ini.html): A higher-kinded type is a type that abstracts over type constructors rather than over concrete types.
[HMAC](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/hmac.ini.html): HMAC, short for Hash-based Message Authentication Code, is a mechanism for message authentication using cryptographic hash functions.
[Hoisting](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/hoisting.ini.html): Hoisting is a language feature where declarations are automatically moved to the top of their scope before code execution.
[Homoiconicity](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/homoiconicity.ini.html): Homoiconicity is a property of a programming language in which code and data share the same representation.
[Honey Pot](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/honeypot.ini.html): A honeypot is a spam-prevention technique used in forms.
[Hook](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/hook.ini.html): A hook is a mechanism that allows custom code to be inserted at a predefined point in a process, framework, or system.
[Horizontal Scaling](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/horizontal-scaling.ini.html): Horizontal scaling, also called scaling out, means adding more instances of an application or service to distribute the workload across multiple machines, rather than increasing the resources of a single machine.
[Hosting](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/hosting.ini.html): Hosting is a server that stores the application code, and is accessible to a audience for usage.
[hrtime()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/hrtime.ini.html): ``hrtime()`` is a native PHP function and an PIE extension.
[.htaccess](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/htaccess.ini.html): The .
[HTML Entities](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/html-entities.ini.html): HTML entities are a way to write special characters.
[HTML Entity](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/html-entity.ini.html): HTML entities are special character sequences, that represents a single character.
[HyperText Markup Language (HTML)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/html.ini.html): HyperText Markup Language, or HTML is the standard language used to create and structure content on the web.
[html_entity_decode](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/html_entity_decode.ini.html): ``html_entity_decode()`` is a built-in PHP function that converts HTML entities back to their corresponding characters.
[html_errors](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/html_errors.ini.html): When ``html_errors`` is enabled, the error message includes HTML tags, to be included in HTML pages.
[htmlentities()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/htmlentities.ini.html): ``htmlentities()`` is a native PHP function which convert special characters in a string to their equivalent in HTML.
[htmlspecialchars](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/htmlspecialchars.ini.html): ``htmlspecialchars()`` is a built-in PHP function that converts special characters to their HTML entity equivalents.
[HTTP Code](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/http-code.ini.html): HTTP status codes are three-digit numeric codes that are returned by web servers to indicate the outcome of a client's request to a server.
[HTTP Encoding](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/http-encoding.ini.html): URL encoding, also known as percent-encoding, is a mechanism used to convert characters into a format that can be transmitted over the Internet.
[HTTP Headers](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/http-header.ini.html): HTTP headers are extra information, exchanged between the server and the client, to configure further the network transaction.
[HTTP Request Headers](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/http-headers.ini.html): HTTP request headers are sent by the client to the server at the start of an HTTP request.
[HTTP Method](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/http-method.ini.html): An HTTP method, also called an HTTP verb, defines the intended action of an HTTP request.
[Hyper Text Transfer Protocol (HTTP)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/http.ini.html): The Hypertext Transfer Protocol, or http, is an application layer protocol in the Internet protocol.
[http_build_query()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/http_build_query.ini.html): The ``http_build_query()`` native function is used to generate a URL-encoded query string from an associative array or an object.
[Hyper Text Transfer Protocol Secure (HTTPS)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/https.ini.html): Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure, or HTTPS, is an extension of the Hypertext Transfer Protocol, HTTP.
[Hydratation](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/hydrate.ini.html): To hydrate is to fill an object with data coming from an unstructured format, before using it.
[Hygienic Macro](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/hygienic-macro.ini.html): A hygienic macro system is one in which macro expansions cannot accidentally capture or shadow variables from the calling scope.
[hypot()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/hypot.ini.html): ``hypot()`` is a PHP native function, that computes the length of the hypotenuse on a right triangle, given the length of the two other sides.
[IaaS](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/iaas.ini.html): IaaS, Infrastructure as a Service, is a cloud-computing model in which a provider supplies virtualized computing resources, such as servers, storage, and networking, over the internet, on demand and billed by usage.
[Infrastructure as Code (IaC)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/iac.ini.html): Infrastructure as Code, aka IaC, is the practice of managing and provisioning infrastructure through machine-readable configuration files rather than manual processes or interactive configuration tools.
[Iconv](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/iconv.ini.html): ``iconv`` is a PHP extension that stands for "Internationalized CONVersion".
[Integrated Environment of Development (IDE)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/ide.ini.html): An IDE is a development tools which thrive to gather all needed tools in one place to allow for a full development experience.
[Idempotent](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/idempotent.ini.html): Idempotent is said of an operation, which yields always the same result after the first call.
[Identical Operator](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/identical.ini.html): Identical is the state of two variables, which contains the same data, with the same type.
[Identification](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/identification.ini.html): Identification refers to the process of recognizing who the user is, usually by having them provide a unique identifier.
[Identifier](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/identifier.ini.html): An identifier is simply a name used to identify a function, class, enum, interface, trait, method, constant, or class constant.
[Identity Comparison](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/identity-comparison.ini.html): The identity comparison ``===`` compares two values, using their respective types, and then, their value.
[Identity](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/identity.ini.html): Identity has several related but distinct meanings:
[If Then Else](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/if-then.ini.html): A ``if-then-else`` command branches the execution based on an condition.
[Iffectation](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/iffectation.ini.html): An iffection is both an assignation and a condition at the same time.
[Image Injection](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/image-injection.ini.html): Image injection is a class of attack where malicious content is embedded inside a file that is presented or processed as an image.
[Image](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/image.ini.html): Images may be different things:
[imagick](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/imagick.ini.html): ``imagick`` is the native PHP extension to create and modify images using the ImageMagick library.
[ImagickException](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/imagickexception.ini.html): The ``ImagickException`` exception is thrown when an error happens while processing an ``Imagick`` operation.
[ImagickPixelException](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/imagickpixelexception.ini.html): ``ImagickPixelException`` is an exception class that is used to handle exceptions related to problems with ``ImagickPixel`` objects.
[IMAP](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/imap.ini.html): ``IMAP``, for Internet Message Access Protocol, is a PHP extension that provides functions to connect to mail servers over IMAP, POP3, and NNTP, list and search mailboxes, fetch message headers and bodies, and manage flags such as read, deleted, or flagged.
[Immutable](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/immutable.ini.html): An immutable object is an object that can't be changed.
[Imperative Programming](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/imperative.ini.html): Declarative programming is the programming style where each step is described to the programm, which then, executes them one after the other.
[implements](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/implements.ini.html): ``implements`` is a keyword, dedicated to classes.
[Implicit State](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/implicit-state.ini.html): Implicit state is state that a function or method relies on without declaring it as a parameter or return value.
[Implicit](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/implicit.ini.html): A feature is implicit when it is available, yet not explicitly visible.
[implode()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/implode.ini.html): The ``implode()`` function is used to join elements of an array into a glue, placed between each element.
[Import](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/import.ini.html): Imports are elements of a different namespaces, brought into the current one.
[Impure Function](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/impure-function.ini.html): An impure function is a function that has at least one of the following characteristics:
[in_array()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/in_array.ini.html): ``in_array()`` function checks if a value exists in an array.
[include](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/include.ini.html): Inclusion, often represented by the ``include`` keyword, is the process to read an external file, and evaluate with the current application for execution.
[include_path](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/include_path.ini.html): The ``include_path`` directive sets the directories where the file functions look for files to read or write files.
[Inclusions](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/inclusion.ini.html): Inclusion is the operation to add PHP code stored in an external file, into another PHP code.
[Incoming Data](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/incoming-data.ini.html): Incoming data are data submitted to PHP by the user.
[Inconsistent](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/inconsistent.ini.html): Inconsistent is a trait of written code, where different conventions or style are used at the same time.
[Increment](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/increment.ini.html): Increment refers to the process of increasing or adding a small amount to a value, or simply one unit.
[Indentation](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/indentation.ini.html): Indentation is a convention governing the layout of blocks of code to convey program structure.
[Index For Arrays](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/index-array.ini.html): A index is the identifier of an specific element in an array.
[Index For SQL](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/index-sql.ini.html): In SQL, an index is a database object that improves the performance of data retrieval operations on database tables.
[Index](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/index.ini.html): An index may be several things:
[Indexed Array](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/indexed-array.ini.html): An indexed array is an array whose keys are only integers.
[Inequality](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/inequality.ini.html): Inequalities are the mathematical comparison of values, with the ``>``,  ``>=``,  ``<`` and ``=<`` operators.
[INF](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/inf.ini.html): Infinite is a PHP constant, called ``INF``.
[Infinite Loop](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/infinite-loop.ini.html): An infinite loop never ends, unless there is an external event.
[Infinite](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/infinite.ini.html): Infinite means that an operation is repeated and will never stop being repeated.
[InfiniteIterator](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/infiniteiterator.ini.html):  The InfiniteIterator allows one to infinitely iterate over an iterator without having to manually rewind the iterator upon reaching its end.
[Inflector](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/inflector.ini.html): An inflector processes strings to adapt them for various situations, such as singular/plural, or for uppercase/lowercase.
[Inheritance](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/inheritance.ini.html): Inheritance is a mechanism where a class is build from another class, and form a hierarchy of classes that share a set of attributes and methods.
[Inherited Variable](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/inherited-variable.ini.html): An inherited variable is a variable that was passed to a closure via the ``use`` clause in the closure definition.
[INI](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/ini.ini.html): The INI format is a simple, human-readable file format used for configuration settings.
[ini_set()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/ini_set.ini.html): ``ini_set()`` is a PHP native function which can change PHP directive, during execution.
[Initialisation](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/initialisation.ini.html): Initialisation is the process to unconditionally give a value to a variable, or any container, before its usage.
[Injection](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/injection.ini.html): Injection is the usage of a resource, such as data, object or services, that is provided to the method, by the caller.
[Inline Assembly](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/inline-assembly.ini.html): Inline assembly is a language feature, found in C, C++, and Rust, that lets raw processor assembly instructions be embedded directly inside otherwise ordinary source code, such as GCC's ``asm(".
[Inlining](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/inlining.ini.html): Inlining is the process to bring a dependency inside the current code, so as to remove that dependency.
[Insecure Deserialization](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/insecure-deserialization.ini.html): Insecure deserialization is a vulnerability that occurs when untrusted data is deserialized without sufficient validation, allowing an attacker to manipulate the serialized object to execute arbitrary code.
[instance](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/instance.ini.html): An instance is an object of a class.
[instanceof](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/instanceof.ini.html): ``instanceof`` is used to determine whether a PHP variable is an instantiated object of a certain class.
[Insteadof](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/insteadof.ini.html): The insteadof operator is used in the context of trait composition and resolving conflicts between methods or properties of multiple traits that are being used in a class.
[Instruction](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/instruction.ini.html): The term instruction most commonly refers to a PHP language statement, such as an assignment, a function call, or a control structure.
[intdiv()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/intdiv.ini.html): ``intdiv()`` is a PHP native function, that computes the integer division between two integer.
[integer](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/integer.ini.html): An integer, or ``int`` is a number of the set ℤ = ``{.
[Integration Test](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/integration-test.ini.html): Integration tests are tests that verify how multiple components or packages work together, as opposed to unit tests, where test are dedicated to one component.
[Interface](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/interface.ini.html): Object interfaces allow to create code which specifies which methods a class must implement, without having to define how these methods are implemented.
[Internals](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/internals.ini.html): In PHP, internals refers to everything under the hood of the language itself, not the code written with PHP.
[Internationalization](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/internationalization.ini.html): Internationalization is the process to make the human interface of a program in a local language.
[Internet](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/internet.ini.html): The internet is a global network of interconnected computers and devices that communicate using shared protocols.
[Interoperability](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/interoperability.ini.html): Interoperability is the ability of distinct software components or systems to exchange information and work together effectively, without special adaptation effort.
[Interpolation](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/interpolation.ini.html): Interpolation is the PHP feature that automatically replaces variables and public properties by their value in double-quoted strings and Heredoc syntax.
[Interpreted](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/interpreted.ini.html): An interpreted language is a programming language in which the source code is executed line by line by another program called an interpreter, rather than being converted directly into machine code by a compiler.
[Intersection Type](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/intersection-type.ini.html): Intersection types, also called intersectional types, is a specification syntax where several types act together as a single type.
[Internationalization Functions](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/intl.ini.html): The internationalization extension performs operations with locales.
[Introspection](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/introspection.ini.html): Introspection is the ability to examine the properties and behavior of objects, classes, and functions at runtime.
[InvalidArgumentException](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/invalidargumentexception.ini.html): Exception thrown if an argument is not of the expected type.
[Type Invariant](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/invariant.ini.html): A type invariant is a data container whose type can't change between a parent class and a child class.
[Code Inventory](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/inventory.ini.html): A code inventory is the collection of all elements of the same type, for categorized review.
[Invoke](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/invoke.ini.html): It is possible to invoke an object, that means calling a function built with the object as the function name.
[Internet of Things (IoT)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/iot.ini.html): IoT, the Internet of Things, refers to the network of physical devices, such as sensors, actuators, vehicles, appliances, industrial machines, etc.
[Internet Protocol (IP)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/ip.ini.html): Internet Protocol:
[is_a()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/is_a.ini.html): ``is_a()`` is a PHP native function, that checks if an object is of a specific class.
[is_array()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/is_array.ini.html): ``is_array()`` is a PHP native function that checks whether a variable is of type array.
[is_object()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/is_object.ini.html): ``is_object()`` is a PHP native function that checks if the content of a variable is an object or not, without consideration for the actual class.
[Interface Segregation Principle (ISP)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/isp.ini.html): The interface segregation principle, or ISP, states that no code should be forced to depend on methods it does not use.
[Isset](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/isset.ini.html): ``isset()`` is a function that determines if a variable is declared and is different than ``null``.
[Iterable](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/iterable.ini.html): Iterable is a pseudo-type, which accepts any array or object implementing the ``Traversable`` interface.
[Iterator](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/iterator.ini.html): An iterator is an object which can be traversed with a ``foreach()`` loop.
[iterator_to_array()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/iterator_to_array.ini.html): ``iterator_to_array()`` converts Iterators and Generators into an array.
[IteratorAggregate](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/iteratoraggregate.ini.html): ``IteratorAggregate`` is an interface to create a custom iterator.
[Javascript](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/javascript.ini.html): JavaScript is a programming language primarily used to make websites interactive.
[Just In Time (JIT)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/jit.ini.html): Just-In-Time compilation is a technique used in computer science and programming languages to improve the runtime performance of code execution.
[Jobqueue](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/jobqueue.ini.html): A job queue is a concept that refers to a system or data structure used to manage and schedule the execution of various tasks or jobs in a sequential or parallel manner.
[JPEG](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/jpg.ini.html): JPEG, the Joint Photographic Experts Group, is a lossy image compression format widely used for photographs and complex images.
[JavaScript Object Notation (JSON)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/json.ini.html): JSON, or JavaScript Object Notation, is a lightweight data-interchange format.
[json_decode()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/json_decode.ini.html): ``json_decode()`` decodes a JSON string into a data structure.
[json_encode()](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/json_encode.ini.html): ``json_encode()`` encodes a data structure into a JSON string.
[JSON Lines (JSONC)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/jsonc.ini.html): JSONC (JSON with Comments) is a superset of JSON that extends its functionality by allowing single-line ``//`` and multi-line ``/* */`` comments.
[JsonException](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/jsonexception.ini.html): Exception thrown if ``JSON_THROW_ON_ERROR`` option is set for ``json_encode()`` or ``json_decode()``.
[JSON Lines (JSONL)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/jsonl.ini.html): JSONL, JSON Lines, is a text-based format where each line is a valid, independent JSON object, separated by newline characters.
[JsonSerializable](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/jsonserializable.ini.html): The ``JsonSerializable`` interface is used to customize how an object is serialized into JSON using ``json_encode()``.
[Jump](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/jump.ini.html): A jump typically refers to any non-linear control flow, where the program skips from one part of the code to another.
[JSON Web Token (JWT)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/jwt.ini.html): A JSON Web Token, JWT, is a compact, URL-safe token format defined in RFC 7519, used to securely transmit claims between parties as a JSON object.
[Key-value Stores](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/key-value-store.ini.html): A key value store is a database which stores data like a hash.
[Keyword](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/keyword.ini.html): PHP has a list of keywords, which are also called reserved names.
[KISS](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/kiss.ini.html): KISS stands for ``Keep It Simple, Stupid``.
[Kubernetes](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/kubernetes.ini.html): Kubernetes, nicknamed k8s, is an open-source container orchestration platform that automates the deployment, scaling, networking, and lifecycle management of containerised applications.
[Goto Labels](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/label.ini.html): Labels are the receiving end of a goto call.
[Linux Apache Mysql PHP (LAMP)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/lamp.ini.html): LAMP is a popular open-source software stack used to build and deploy web applications.
[Language Construct](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/language-construct.ini.html): PHP has a notion of ``language construct``, which are special keywords, with an attached feature.
[LSP](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/language-service-protocol.ini.html): A Language Server Protocol, an LSP server, is a program that provides language-intelligence features to any code editor and IDE that speaks the LSP standard.
[Laravel](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/laravel.ini.html): Laravel is a popular PHP framework used for building web applications.
[Late Static Binding](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/late-static-binding.ini.html): PHP implements a feature called late static bindings which can be used to reference the called class in a context of static inheritance.
[Latency](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/latency.ini.html): Latency is the delay between a request being made and the response being received.
[Latin1](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/latin1.ini.html): ``Latin1``, also called ``ISO/IEC 8859-1`` and ``iso-8859-1``, is a character encoding.
[Layered Architecture](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/layered-architecture.ini.html): Layered architecture organises a codebase into horizontal layers, each with a specific responsibility, where each layer only depends on the layer directly below it.
[Lazy Evaluation](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/lazy-evaluation.ini.html): Lazy evaluation is an evaluation strategy in which an expression's value is not computed until it is actually needed, and, once computed, is often cached so it is never recomputed.
[Lazy Loading](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/lazy-loading.ini.html): Lazy loading is a programming pattern that postpone the actual loading of a resource to the moment it is actually used.
[Lazy Objects](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/lazy-objects.ini.html): Lazy objects is a application of lazy-loading, directly available in the PHP engine, and available as an engine feature since PHP 8.
[LDAP Injection](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/ldap-injection.ini.html): LDAP injection is a security vulnerability that occurs when untrusted user input is incorporated into an LDAP query without proper sanitisation or escaping.
[Lightweight Directory Access Protocol (LDAP)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/ldap.ini.html): LDAP, acronym of Lightweight Directory Access Protocol, is an open, vendor-neutral, industry standard application protocol for accessing and maintaining distributed directory information services over an Internet Protocol, IP network.
[Leading Numeric String](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/leading-numeric-string.ini.html): A leading numeric string is subset of numeric string, where the first characters are numbers (or the occasional + or -), and the rest of the string is non digits.
[Leading Zero Means Octal](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/leading-zero-means-octal.ini.html): PHP integer syntax has some special formats for binary, octal and hexadecimal formats.
[Leak](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/leak.ini.html): A leak generally refers to a situation where resources are not properly released after they should have been.
[Legacy](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/legacy.ini.html): Legacy code refers to existing code written under older practices, PHP versions, or conventions that are no longer considered current.
[Linux Nginx Mysql PHP (LEMP)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/lemp.ini.html): LEMP is a popular open-source software stack used to build and deploy web applications.
[LengthException](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/lengthexception.ini.html): The LengthException exception is thrown if a length is invalid.
[Letter Logical Bug](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/letter-logical.ini.html): PHP offers two sets of operators for logical operations:
[Lexical Analysis](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/lexical-analysis.ini.html): Lexical analysis, also called tokenisation or scanning, is the first phase of a compiler or static analyser.
[Lexical Variable](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/lexical.ini.html): A lexical variable is the type of variable that appears in the ``use`` part of a closure definition.
[Library Loading](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/library-loading.ini.html): PHP has a modular structure, which allows it to load extensions.
[Library](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/library.ini.html): A library is a collection of reusable code, functions, classes, or constants, that provides specific functionality and can be included in different applications.
[libsodium](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/libsodium.ini.html): Sodium is a modern, easy-to-use software library for encryption, decryption, signatures, password hashing and more.
[Licence](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/licence.ini.html): The licence refers to legal terms under which a source code is distributed and can be used.
[Lifecycle](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/lifecycle.ini.html): The lifecycle of an application spans from initial development to decommissioning, covering stages like specifications, prototyping, coding, testing, deployment, maintenance, and eventual retirement.
[Last In, First Out (LIFO)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/lifo.ini.html): Last In, First Out is the principle of management of a stack.
[Line Feed](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/line-feed.ini.html): A line feed is a whitespace character, that historically represents moving the cursor to the next line, without going back to the beginning of the line.
[Linear Type](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/linear-type.ini.html): Linear types and affine types are type disciplines that constrain how many times a value may be used.
[Link](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/link.ini.html): A link connects two resources, allowing navigation or reference from one to the other.
[Lint, Won't Execute](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/lint-wont-execute.ini.html): ``Lint, won't execute`` is a special state of the code, where the source can be parsed, but cannot be executed.
[Linting](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/linting.ini.html): Linting is the process to turn the Text file that holds the source code, into tokens.
[Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/liskov.ini.html): The Liskov Substitution Principle states that an object from a class and a sub-object, from a class that extends that object, must be interchangeable without breaking the program.
[List Comprehension](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/list-comprehension.ini.html): A list comprehension is an expression-level syntax for building a new collection by describing it declaratively:
[List](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/list.ini.html): ``list()`` acts as the contrary to ``array()``:
[Listener](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/listener.ini.html): A listener is a callable function that is registered to handle a specific event or message when it is dispatched.
[Literal Types](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/literal-types.ini.html): Literal types are type which not only check the type of a value but also the value itself.
[Literal](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/literal.ini.html): A literal is a hard coded value in the source.
[Large Language Model (LLM)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/llm.ini.html): An LLM, or Large Language Model, is a type of artificial intelligence model designed to understand, generate, and interact with human language.
[Load Balancer](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/load-balancer.ini.html): A load balancer is a component that distributes incoming network traffic across multiple backend servers to improve availability, throughput, and fault tolerance.
[Local File Inclusion](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/local-file-inclusion.ini.html): A local file inclusion is a security vulnerability, where an external attacker can choose which file from the local system is processed by the called script.
[Local Variable](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/local-variable.ini.html): A local variable is a variable in a method or a function.
[Local Scope](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/local.ini.html): The local scope is the scope inside a function or method.
[Locale](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/locale.ini.html): Locale are a set of configuration which describe how to handle different formats of data, such as monetary values, dates and time.
[Lock](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/lock.ini.html): A lock is a mechanism used to control access to a shared resource so that only one process (or script) can use it at a time.
[Log File](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/log-file.ini.html): Log is a list of event that happen during execution of a piece of software.
[Log](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/log.ini.html): Log has several meanings:
[Logarithm](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/logarithm.ini.html): The logarithm is a family of mathematical functions, where ``log($a * $b) == log($a) + log($b)``.
[Logical Operators](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/logical-operator.ini.html): Logical operators applies four logical functions to booleans.
[LogicException](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/logicexception.ini.html): Exception that represents error in the program logic.
[Look-up](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/look-up.ini.html): A look-up is the operation of retrieving a value from a data structure by key, index, or identity.
[Loops](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/loop.ini.html): A loop is a control structure that allows to execute a block of code repeatedly based on a specified condition.
[Lossless](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/lossless.ini.html): Lossless refers to any encoding, compression, or conversion process that preserves all of the original data exactly.
[Lossy](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/lossy.ini.html): Lossy refers to any encoding, compression, or conversion process that permanently discards some of the original data in order to reduce size or simplify the representation.
[LSP](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/lsp.ini.html): LSP is an acronym that has several meanings:
[Machine Learning](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/machine-learning.ini.html): Machine learning is a field of artificial intelligence, which teaches an application how to react to a vast number of situations with a training dataset, before applying these teachings on new situations.
[Macro](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/macro.ini.html): A macro is a rule or pattern that maps a source text or syntax fragment to a replacement, which is then substituted before or during compilation.
[Magento](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/magento.ini.html): Magento is an open-source PHP e-commerce platform.
[Magic Bytes](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/magic-byte.ini.html): Magic bytes are the first bytes of the content of a file.
[Magic Constants](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/magic-constant.ini.html): Constants which hold information about the current location of the code.
[Magic Hash](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/magic-hash.ini.html): A magic hash is a hash string which is mistakenly compared to another hash.
[Magic Code](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/magic-in-the-code.ini.html): Magic in the code happens when a simple command execute a lot more than it seems.
[Magic Methods](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/magic-method.ini.html): Special methods, which provide extended support for PHP features.
[Magic Numbers](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/magic-number.ini.html): A magic number is a number that appears in the code, and has no obvious signification.
[Magic Property](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/magic-property.ini.html): Special properties, which are not declared, but dynamically handled by the magic methods.
[Magic Values](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/magic-value.ini.html): A magic value is a literal value that appears in the code without a clear explanation of its meaning.
[Magic](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/magic.ini.html): Magic is mostly used in the sentence:
[Mail](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/mail.ini.html): ``mail()`` is the PHP native function that sends emails.
[Maintenability](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/maintenability.ini.html): Maintainability refers to how easy it is to understand, modify, and extend source code over time, especially as the project grows or / and requirements change.
[Maintenance](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/maintenance.ini.html): Maintenance is the operation to keep a source code running, long time after it was created.
[Makefile](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/makefile.ini.html): A ``Makefile`` is a configuration file used by the ``make`` build automation tool.
[Man In The Middle](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/man-in-the-middle.ini.html): A Man-in-the-Middle attack, aka MitM, is a cyberattack where a malicious actor intercepts and possibly alters communications between two parties without their knowledge.
[Map](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/map.ini.html): A map refers to an abstract data type, ADT, that stores key-value pairs, where each key is unique within the map.
[Markdown](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/markdown.ini.html): Markdown is a lightweight markup language that can be used to add formatting elements to plain text documents.
[Marketplace](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/marketplace.ini.html): A marketplace usually means a platform where developers can buy, sell, or distribute PHP or framework related products or services.
[Match](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/match.ini.html): The ``match`` expression is the remote cousin of the ``switch``.
[Math](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/math.ini.html): PHP offers many math features.
[max_execution_time](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/max_execution_time.ini.html): The ``max_execution_time`` directive sets the maximum amount of time that PHP will work on an application.
[Multibyte String](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/mbstring.ini.html): ``mbstring`` provides function to process multibyte strings functions.
[Model Context Protocol (MCP)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/mcp.ini.html): An MCP server usually refers to a server that implements the Model Context Protocol, or MCP.
[mcrypt Extension](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/mcrypt.ini.html): ``mcrypt`` is a PHP extension that provides access to the mcrypt library.
[Message Digest Algorithm 5 (MD5)](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/md5.ini.html): MD5 is a hash function, that produce a 128-bit footprint for a value.
[Memoization](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/memoization.ini.html): Memoization is an optimisation technique to speed up a function by avoiding re-processing previously calculated results.
[Memory Arena](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/memory-arena.ini.html): A memory arena is a large, contiguous block of memory that is pre-allocated upfront and then carved into smaller pieces on demand.
[Memory Leak](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/memory-leak.ini.html): A memory leak is the allocation of memory, without its deallocation when that memory is not needed anymore.
[Memory](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/memory.ini.html): Memory refers to the portion of server RAM that PHP uses to execute scripts.
[memory_limit](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/memory_limit.ini.html): ``memory_limit`` is the directive that caps the maximum amount of memory that PHP can use.
[Merge](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/merge.ini.html): To merge is the action of combining two things into one.
[Message Bus](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/message-bus.ini.html): A message bus is a communication mechanism that allows different components or services to exchange messages in a decoupled manner.
[Message Digest](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/message-digest.ini.html): A message digest is the fixed-size output of a cryptographic hash function, applied to an arbitrary piece of data, called the message.
[Message Queue](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/message-queue.ini.html): A message queue server is a middleware component or service that facilitates communication between different software components or systems by enabling asynchronous message passing.
[Meta-object Protocol](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/meta-object-protocol.ini.html): A Meta-object Protocol (MOP) is an API that exposes a language's own object system as a first-class, programmable layer.
[Metaclass](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/metaclass.ini.html): A metaclass is a class whose instances are themselves classes.
[Metaprogramming](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/metaprogramming.ini.html): Metaprogramming is the practice of writing code that treats other code as data:
[Method Collision](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/method-collision.ini.html): A method collision happens when a trait is imported in a class, and it holds a method that already exists in the class.
[Method Compatibility](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/method-compatibility.ini.html): Method compatibility means that when a class overrides a method, from a parent class or interface, the new method must keep a compatible signature.
[Method Overloading](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/method-overloading.ini.html): Method overloading is a feature that allows multiple definitions of a method, depending on the type of the passed parameters.
[Method Signature](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/method-signature.ini.html): The signature of a method or a function is the list of arguments and constraints that apply when calling it.
[Method](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/method.ini.html): Methods are functions, dedicated to a class.
[Methodcall](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/methodcall.ini.html): A method call is the actual usage of a method.
[Metrics](https://php-dictionary.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dictionary/metrics.ini.html): Metrics refer to quantitative measurements used to evaluate the performance, quality, and behavior of the code.
